original_hypothesis.title: Wallet implementation storage slot collision risk: Implementation pointer stored at slot keyed by address() may be clobbered by delegatecalled hooks/extensions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Implementation._setImplementation stores the proxy's implementation address at storage slot `sstore(address(), _imp)` (i.e., slot == uint256(uint160(address(this))) in the proxy context). Any delegatecall-executed code (Hooks.fallback hook implementations, Calls delegateCall extensions) can write to arbitrary storage including this slot, changing the wallet implementation and taking over execution permanently. While installing hooks is onlySelf (intended privileged), this storage layout is unusually collision-prone: any module using Solidity state variables could accidentally map to this slot under certain compiler layouts, and any mistakenly-installed extension/hook can brick or hijack the wallet.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Phase A notes high collision risk due to non-EIP1967 slot choice. Even without a malicious actor, accidental collisions during future upgrades/modules are realistic. With a malicious hook, takeover is trivial; realism is low because it requires privileged installation, but severity remains high.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet installs third-party hook/extension code via addHook or executes delegateCall=true calls
Hook/extension contains storage writes (intentional or accidental) that can reach slot `address()`
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Hooks system is enabled by default in Stage1Module/Stage2Module; addHook is onlySelf. No further allowlist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Non-standard impl slot equals address(); delegatecall-capable system allows arbitrary storage writes

preconditions: Wallet executes delegatecall into untrusted or buggy code (hook or extension)
That code writes to the implementation slot

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Implementation pointer stored in a non-reserved, collision-prone slot
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Accidental or malicious overwrite of implementation causes permanent takeover/brick
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No EIP-1967 slot; no module sandboxing; no rollback protection

analysis_questions: Are there any shipped hook implementations/extensions that could collide with this slot unintentionally?
Do any delegatecall paths exist besides Hooks.fallback and Calls delegateCall that are reachable in normal operation?
Is there an emergency recovery path if implementation slot is corrupted?
validation_requirements: Compute actual slot index for implementation in proxy context and attempt overwrite via a minimal hook
Review planned/known extensions for storage layout assumptions
Check if updateImplementation can recover from overwrite (requires onlySelf)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User installs a third-party hook; hook delegatecall writes to slot `address()` and changes implementation to attacker-controlled logic, draining assets on next call.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Often none if implementation changed to hostile code; depends on attacker behavior

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No regression tests asserting implementation slot is protected from common storage layouts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: watch for unexpected ImplementationUpdated events / implementation slot diffs

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
